abstract: This paper proposes a simple method for placing political texts on policy and ideological dimensions using instruction-tuned large language models such as GPT-4, Llama 3, MiXtral, and Aya. The approach asks the LLM to locate a tweet or sentence on a focal dimension and averages many such responses to produce position estimates for political actors or longer texts. The authors report high correlations between these LLM-based estimates and established benchmarks across multiple cases and languages.
key_claims: Instruction-tuned LLMs can be used directly, via prompting and averaging, to place political texts in policy and ideological spaces.
Averaging LLM responses across many short texts (tweets, sentences) yields reliable position estimates for political actors and longer documents.
The method generalizes across languages and types of political text, including US Senators' tweets, UK party manifestos, and multilingual EU policy speeches.
methods: Prompting instruction-tuned LLMs (GPT-4, Llama 3, MiXtral, Aya) to rate where a given tweet or sentence falls on a specified policy or ideological dimension.
Aggregating (averaging) the LLM-generated position scores across texts to estimate the position of a political actor or document.
Applying the procedure to multiple corpora: US Senators' tweets, UK party manifestos, and EU policy speeches in 10 languages.
Validating estimates by correlating them with established benchmark measures of political positions.
findings: LLM-derived position estimates correlate strongly with benchmark measures of political positioning.
The approach works across different LLMs, text types, and languages, including in multilingual settings such as EU speeches.
contributions: Introduces a straightforward 'ask and average' methodology for scaling political texts using off-the-shelf instruction-tuned LLMs.
Demonstrates cross-lingual and cross-context applicability for political text analysis without bespoke model training.
Provides empirical validation against established political positioning benchmarks across several case studies.
framing: The paper sits within the political text scaling and text-as-data literature in political science, proposing LLM-based prompting as an alternative to traditional supervised or unsupervised scaling methods like Wordscores or Wordfish. It contributes to a growing conversation about using generative LLMs as measurement tools in the social sciences.
